tool_summary: Builds and executes commands from standard input.
platform_notes: The base POSIX specification defines -I, -L, and -n. Extensions like -0 (null delimiter), -a (arg-file), or -P (parallelism) are common in GNU/BSD but may not be available on all POSIX systems.

rules.description: Invoking a shell (e.g., sh -c) via xargs allows for complex command sequences, pipes, and redirections, significantly increasing the risk of unsafe code execution.
rules.reversible_note: Depends on the commands executed by the shell.
rules.notes: The presence of a shell allows for command injection if input is untrusted.

rules.description: The -P flag enables parallel execution. High values or -P 0 can lead to system resource exhaustion (CPU/RAM DOS), potentially crashing the host.
rules.reversible_note: The reversibility depends on the command executed by xargs, not the parallel execution flag itself.
rules.notes: While not destructive to files, excessive parallelism can lead to Denial of Service.

rules.description: The -a flag (or --arg-file) tells xargs to read input from a specific file instead of stdin.
rules.reversible_note: The reversibility depends on the command executed by xargs, not the source of the arguments.
rules.notes: Changes the input source; risk depends on the commanded tool.

rules.description: The -0 flag specifies that input items are terminated by a null character instead of whitespace. This is typically used in pipelines with find -print0 to safely handle filenames containing spaces.
rules.notes: Signals intent to process bulk filenames with special characters via a pipeline.

rules.description: The -I flag allows substituting a placeholder in the command template with each input line. This can be an injection vector if the input is untrusted.
rules.reversible_note: The reversibility depends on the command executed by xargs.
rules.notes: Precise injection of input into arbitrary command positions can lead to unsafe execution if input is not sanitized.
